6: Jonathan's battle-speech to his armor-bearer is one of the OT's most compressed statements of active faith: ûlᵉyôhônātān kî ʾên laYHWH māḵôr lᵉhôšîaʿ bᵉraḇ ʾô ḇimʿāṭ — 'for nothing prevents YHWH from saving whether by many or by few.' The verb māḵôr (hindrance, restraint) is rare; the theological claim is radical: divine saving action is not constrained by military arithmetic. This anticipates Gideon's 300 (Judg 7), David's single stone against Goliath, and Paul's 'when I am weak, then I am strong' (2 Corinthians 12:10). Jonathan acts from the epistemological inversion that characterizes biblical faith: the normal ratio of forces is irrelevant when YHWH acts. His father Saul, by contrast, is paralyzed by numbers — waiting under a pomegranate tree while his kingdom evaporates (v2).
12: The armor-bearer's response — ʿăśēh kol ʾăšer bilᵉḇāḇeḵā nəṭēh lāḵ hinnᵉnî ʿimmᵉḵā kilᵉḇāḇeḵā — 'Do all that is in your heart; here I am with you according to your heart' — uses the lēḇāḇ vocabulary of 1 Samuel 13:14: the man after the heart, the heart-aligned companion. The phrase hinnᵉnî ʿimmᵉḵā (here I am with you) echoes the divine commissioning formula (ʾehyeh ʿimmāḵ, 'I will be with you') given to Moses, Gideon, and the judges. Jonathan and his armor-bearer form a two-man unit operating in the lēḇāḇ-mode that defines the coming Davidic king — not Saul's paralyzed majority but Jonathan's faithful two.
15: The divine panic at Michmash is described as ḥardat ʾelōhîm — 'the trembling of God' — which even the ground participates in: wattirʿaš hāʾāreṣ, 'the earth shook.' The ḥardat ʾelōhîm is the supernatural terror that YHWH sends before his people to disable the enemy (cf. Exodus 23:27 — 'I will send my terror before you'; Joshua 2:9 — Rahab's report that the terror of Israel has fallen on the Canaanites). It is theophanic panic — the fear that precedes YHWH's presence in battle. The Michmash earthquake is also the beginning of a typological pattern: the earth shakes when YHWH acts decisively for his people (cf. Matthew 27:51; 1 Samuel 28:2 — the earthquake at the crucifixion and resurrection).
24: Saul's oath: ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yōʾkal leḥem ʿad hāʿereb wənikqamtî mēʾōyᵉḇay — 'Cursed is the man who eats bread until evening when I have avenged myself on my enemies.' The word ʾārûr (cursed) is covenant-curse vocabulary, drawing from the Deuteronomic blessings and curses (Deut 27-28). Saul invokes the covenant-curse mechanism to bind his own army, making food-restraint into a military-religious obligation. The result is the people's exhaustion (v31) and the near-death of Jonathan. The rash oath (ʾālāh) that nearly kills his own son is a pattern that Jephthah's daughter represents in the worst possible form (Judg 11) — covenant-curse language deployed without divine authorization, generating tragic consequence. Saul's oath is a smaller-scale Jephthah, with the people ultimately rescuing Jonathan where no one rescued Jephthah's daughter.
45: The people's covenant-rescue of Jonathan: ḥālîlāh kî ʾim yāmût yôhônātān ʾăšer ʿāśāh hayyəšûʿāh haggəḏôlāh hazzōʾt bəyiśrāʾēl — 'Far be it! Jonathan shall not die, for he has worked this great salvation (yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh) in Israel.' The phrase yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh (great salvation) is the same formula used by Samson after his jawbone victory (Judges 15:18) and the language from which yēšûaʿ (Jesus, 'salvation') derives its meaning. The people redeem Jonathan from Saul's death sentence — a son rescued from death by the intervention of the covenant community. The irony is double: the son who worked the salvation that neither Saul nor the army could accomplish is nearly killed by his own father's oath, and is only saved by the same people he delivered.
1: vayhî hayyôm vayyōʾmer yônāṯān ben-šāʾûl ʾel-hannāʿar nōśēʾ kelāyw — 'One day Jonathan son of Saul said to his armor-bearer.' The casual vayhî hayyôm (it happened one day — the idiom for a significant day in Israelite narrative, cf. 2 Kings 4:8,11,18) introduces an act of spontaneous faithfulness. Jonathan's plan was told only to his armor-bearer (hannāʿar nōśēʾ kelāyw — the young man carrying his weapons), not to his father Saul. The word nāʿar (H5288 — young man, servant, armor-bearer) designates the intimate military companion. The secrecy from Saul was deliberate — Jonathan knew his father would not sanction the raid, or perhaps that Saul's fearfulness would prevent any initiative.
2: wĕšāʾûl yôšēb biḵrēh haggibĕʿāh taḥat harimmôn ʾăšer bemigĕrôn — 'Saul was sitting under the pomegranate tree at Migron on the outskirts of Gibeah.' The contrast is deliberate: while Jonathan was about to act, Saul was yôšēb (sitting — H3427, the posture of inaction). Harimmôn ʾăšer bemigĕrôn (the pomegranate at Migron — possibly near Gibeah, cf. Isaiah 10:28) was his post. His entourage: keššēš meʾôt ʾîš (about 600 men) and the priests with the ephod (wahăhiyyāh ʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕpānāyw — Ahijah son of Ahitub was wearing the ephod). The ephod present but unused — Saul had the oracle apparatus but did not consult it, while Jonathan would act on raw faith.
3: waʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕpānāyw — 'Ahijah son of Ahitub... was wearing the ephod.' The priestly genealogy — ʾăḥiyyāh ben-ʾăḥîṭûḇ ʾĕhôḏ bĕnê-pînĕḥās ben-ʿēlî kōhēn YHWH bĕšîlôh (son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod, son of Phinehas son of Eli, the priest of YHWH at Shiloh) — connects the current priesthood directly to the disgraced Elide line. The ʾîkāḇôḏ (H350 — Ichabod, 'where is the glory?') reference recalls the moment in 1 Samuel 4:21-22 when the glory departed from Israel with the ark's capture. The priesthood present with Saul was from the lineage of Eli's failed sons — the contrast with Samuel's prophetic authority was stark. Weʾāmrû yisrāʾēl (the people did not know Jonathan had gone).
4: ûḇên hammaʿbĕrôt ʾăšer biqqēš yônāṯān laʿăḇōr ʿal matsaḇ pĕlishtîm — 'Between the passes through which Jonathan sought to cross to the Philistine garrison.' The geographic precision identifies the specific terrain feature Jonathan would use: šēn hassela miḥṣāḏ ûšēn hassela miḥṣāḇ (a sharp rocky crag on one side, a sharp rocky crag on the other side). The word šēn (H8127 — tooth, sharp point) vividly describes the two precipitous cliffs flanking the Michmash ravine. Their names: bôtsēts (H949 — shining/gleaming crag, the one on the north) and seneh (H5573 — thornbush crag, the one on the south — possibly named after its vegetation). The topography of Wadi Suweinit, identified by Albright and confirmed by survey, matches this description precisely.
5: hassēn miṣṣāpôn lenokah mikĕmāš wehaʾaḥēr mittēmān lenokah geḇaʿ — 'The one crag on the north opposite Michmash, the other on the south opposite Geba.' The verse is a pure topographic orientation: Seneh crag was on the south (near Geba, where Jonathan's group was), and Bozez crag was on the north (near Michmash, where the Philistine garrison stood). For Jonathan to reach the Philistine position, he had to cross the ravine (ascending the Seneh crag from the south) in full view of the enemy. The tactical absurdity of the plan — climbing a cliff face toward an armed garrison — was precisely the kind of action that could only be undertaken in faith that YHWH would act.
7: vayyōʾmer lô nōśēʾ kelāyw ʿăśēh kol-ʾăšer belĕḇāḇĕkhā nĕṭēh-lākhā — 'His armor-bearer said to him: Do all that is in your heart; turn to where you will.' The armor-bearer's response is one of the Bible's finest examples of loyal courage: hinnĕhāh ʾimĕkhā kĕliḇĕḇĕkhā (here I am with you, as is your heart). The phrase kĕliḇĕḇĕkhā (as your heart — complete identification with the commander's purpose) anticipates David's mighty men who shared his heart's vision. The armor-bearer did not ask for a battle plan, tactical analysis, or divine confirmation: he committed himself fully to Jonathan's leadership. This unnamed young man modeled the faith that Jonathan himself embodied — willingness to act when outcomes are uncertain but the commander is trustworthy.
8: vayyōʾmer yônāṯān hinnēh ʾănaḥnû ʿōḇrîm ʾel-hāʾănāšîm — 'Jonathan said: We are crossing over to those men.' The plan with its sign: ûnigĕlînû ʾălêhem (we will reveal ourselves to them). The two possible outcomes: (1) if the Philistines say dommû ʿad higgiʿănû ʾălêkem (stay where you are until we come to you) → that is YHWH's sign that defeat will come on them; (2) if they say ʿălû ʿālênû (come up to us) → that is YHWH's sign that He has given them into Israel's hand. The sign was clever: either outcome was usable as divine guidance. The willingness of the Philistines to invite Jonathan up (v12) fulfilled the positive sign precisely — their taunting confidence became their death warrant.
9: ʾim-kōh yōmĕrû ʾēlênû dommû ʿaḏ higĕʿānû ʾălêkem — 'If they say: Wait until we come to you.' The negative sign: dommû (H1826 from H1826 — be still, wait) was the dismissive command of a superior to an inferior who must wait permission to approach. This response would indicate that YHWH had not given the Philistines into Israel's hand — Jonathan's plan would not proceed. The grammar of the oracle-by-sign reflects the binary question format of Urim and Thummim divination (cf. 1 Samuel 23:11-12 where David asks YHWH two yes/no questions). Jonathan's improvised oracle used the enemy's own words as divine speech — a creative appropriation of the theophanic oracle tradition without access to the formal instruments (the ephod was with Saul, unused, v3).
10: weʾim-kōh yōmĕrû ʿălû ʿālênû weʿālînû — 'If they say: Come up to us — then we will go up.' The positive sign: the Philistines' invitation to come up (ʿălû ʿālênû) would be the sign that YHWH had given them into Israel's hand (kî-nĕtānām YHWH bĕyādēnû). The divine irony: the Philistines' contemptuous taunt (they saw only two Israelites — v12 says 'the Hebrews are coming out of their holes') became the oracle that sealed their own doom. Jonathan interpreted their words as YHWH's speech — a reading that requires deep faith in divine sovereignty over the enemy's mouth. The fulfillment: the Philistines said exactly the expected words, and Jonathan's faith was vindicated.
11: vayyiggālû šĕnêhem ʾel-matsaḇ pĕlishtîm — 'Both of them revealed themselves to the Philistine garrison.' The verb gālāh (H1540 — to reveal, expose, uncover) in the reflexive/Niphal means they deliberately showed themselves — stepped out from concealment into the enemy's view. The Philistine response confirmed the sign: hinnēh ʿiḇrîm yōtsĕʾîm min-haḥăḥārîm ʾăšer hiṯḥabbĕʾû šām (look, Hebrews coming out of the holes where they were hiding themselves). The contemptuous reference to the holes (ḥăḥārîm — cf. 13:6 where Israel hid in holes) shows the Philistines' complete disdain for the two Israelites. Their contempt made them careless — and carefulness was the only thing that might have stopped Jonathan.
13: vayyaʿal yônāṯān ʿal-yādāyw weʿal-ragĕlāyw — 'Jonathan climbed up on his hands and feet.' The physical description — hands and feet (yadāyw weragĕlāyw) — portrays Jonathan literally crawling up the cliff face. This is combat reporting of exceptional realism: the great warrior in undignified, vulnerable posture, ascending a rock face under enemy observation. Wenōśēʾ kelāyw ʾaḥărāyw (his armor-bearer after him) followed the same route. Then the climactic sequence: vayyippĕlû lifnê yônāṯān wenōśēʾ kelāyw mĕmôtēt ʾaḥărāyw — the Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer killed behind him. The two-man assault became a one-sided rout: Jonathan struck down the enemy, his companion finished those who fell.
14: vayhî hammakkāh hāriʾšônāh ʾăšer hikkāh yônāṯān weʾet-nōśēʾ kelāyw — 'That first strike by Jonathan and his armor-bearer was about twenty men.' The precision: kĕʿeśrîm ʾîš kĕḥătsiʾ maʿănāh ʾăšer-yeḥrōš-śādeh (about twenty men within half a furrow of land a team plows in a day). The agricultural measurement — half a furrow (ḥătsiʾ maʿănāh) of land plowed in a day — is an unusual military measurement unit, suggesting this was a relatively compact area of a terraced hillside. Twenty trained warriors killed in a confined space by two men: the casualty rate was extraordinary and could only be explained by divine intervention. The scale of the immediate kill prepared for the larger supernatural intervention that followed.
16: vayyirʾû haṣṣopîm lĕšāʾûl begibĕʿat binyāmîn — 'The watchmen of Saul at Gibeah of Benjamin saw.' Haṣṣopîm (H6822 — the lookouts, watchmen) at Saul's position observed the Philistine camp dissolving: wehinnēh hehāmôn nāmôg vayyēlek wahālōm (the multitude was melting and going here and there). The word hāmôn (H1995 — multitude, tumult, roar) for the Philistine camp captures both the numbers and the noise. The verb nāmôg (H4127 — to melt, dissolve — used for the melting of wax, Psalms 68:2; the dissolving of the Canaanites' courage, Joshua 2:24) describes the Philistine rout as a supernatural liquefying of their military resolve.
17: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl lapĕqûḏû nāʾ ûrʾû mî hālaḵ mē ʿimmānû — 'Saul said: Count now and see who has gone from us.' The forensic procedure: pāqaḏ (H6485 — to muster, count, inspect) was the standard military census verb (Numbers 1:3; 1 Samuel 11:8). Only when the rout was already visible did Saul investigate — his leadership pattern was reactive rather than proactive. The discovery: wayyipqĕḏû wehinnēh ʾên yônāṯān weʾet-nōśēʾ kelāyw (when they counted, Jonathan and his armor-bearer were not there). Jonathan's unauthorized raid had already won the battle that Saul was still trying to understand.
18: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl laʾăḥiyyāh haggîšāh ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm — 'Saul said to Ahijah: Bring the ark of God.' The MT reads 'bring the ark of God' but the LXX reads 'bring the ephod' (consistent with v3 — Ahijah wore the ephod, not the ark). The ephod reading is likely original: the ark was at Kiriath-jearim (1 Samuel 7:1), not with Saul's army. The oracle consultation: Saul finally sought YHWH's guidance after the battle had already begun to turn. The phrase kî hāyāh ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm bayyôm hahûʾ ûḇnê yisrāʾēl (for the ark of God was there that day with the children of Israel) suggests the consultation was triggered by the observed rout. But even this delayed consultation would be interrupted (v19: 'withdraw your hand').
19: vayhî ʿad-dabbēr šāʾûl ʾel-hakkōhēn wehahhāmôn ʾăšer-bemahănēh pĕlishtîm — 'While Saul was speaking to the priest, the tumult in the Philistine camp increased.' Wayhî ʾad dabbēr (while speaking/as speaking — the temporal conjunction marking interruption) sets up the contrast: Saul consulting the priest, but the battle going on without him. Wayyāsep wĕhālaḵ (increased and spread). Saul's command: ʾesōp yādêkhā (withdraw your hand — stop the consultation) suggests that Saul decided to skip the oracle and act immediately. The irony: Saul abandoned the divine oracle at exactly the moment he should have used it — his impulsive decision-making versus the patient inquiry that Samuel would have required.
20: vayyizzāʿēq šāʾûl wekhol-hāʿām ʾăšer ʿimmô vayyābōʾû ʿad-hammilḥāmāh — 'Saul and all the people with him rallied and came to the battle.' The Hebrew zaʿaq (H2199 — to cry out, muster with a shout) indicates the battle-cry that mobilized Saul's 600-man force. Wehinnēh hāyĕṯāh ḥereḇ ʾîš berēʿēhû mĕhûmāh gĕḏôlāh meʾōd (every man's sword was against his fellow in very great confusion). The mĕhûmāh (H4103 — panic, confusion, tumult — specifically the supernatural terror YHWH sends into armies, Deuteronomy 7:23; 2 Chronicles 15:5; Zechariah 14:13) was the divine weapon: YHWH caused the Philistines to attack each other. This was the same divine confusion that struck the Midianites for Gideon (Judges 7:22) — YHWH's standard military intervention method when Israel was outnumbered.
21: wĕhāʿiḇrîm hāyû lappĕlishtîm kĕʾetmôl šilšôm — 'The Hebrews who had been with the Philistines previously.' The sociological detail is striking: some Israelites (ʿiḇrîm — Hebrews) had been serving with the Philistine army (hāyû lappĕlishtîm — they were among/for the Philistines) in the period before the battle. This reflects the reality of Israel's subjugation: some had gone over to the Philistine side, perhaps as mercenaries, laborers, or under compulsion. When they saw the divine rout (vayyāsēbbû gam-hēmmāh lihyôt ʿim-yisrāʾēl — they too turned to be with Israel), they switched sides. The battle had a conversion effect: Israelite collaborators with the enemy became fighters for their own people.
22: wĕkhol ʾîš yisrāʾēl hammiṯḥabbĕʾîm bĕhar ʾeprayim — 'All the men of Israel who had hidden in the hill country of Ephraim.' The reference back to the hiding episode of 13:6 creates a narrative arc: the Israelites who had hidden in caves and thickets now heard that the Philistines had fled (vayyišmĕʿû kî-nāsû pĕlishtîm vayyirdĕpû gam-hēmmāh ʾaḥărêhem — they heard the Philistines fled and they also pursued). The transformation from hiding to pursuing is the measure of the divine intervention: the same people who had been paralyzed by fear were now aggressive fighters. YHWH's battle had converted a demoralized refuge-seeking people into a victorious pursuing army.
23: vayyôšaʿ YHWH bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet-yisrāʾēl — 'YHWH saved Israel that day.' The theological summary with the divine subject: vayyôšaʿ YHWH (YHWH saved/delivered — H3467 from yāšaʿ, the root of yĕšûʿāh, salvation). The battle was attributed explicitly to divine action, not to Jonathan's military prowess. Wehammaḥălôqâ ʿāḇrāh ʾet-bêt ʾāwen (the battle passed beyond Beth-aven). The geographical movement of the battle — from Michmash northward past Beth-aven (possibly near Bethel or Ai) — shows the scope of the Philistine rout. The divine name as subject of yāšaʿ recurs in Israel's battle theology (Exodus 14:30; Judges 7:7; 2 Kings 19:34-35), always asserting that military victories were covenant acts, not human achievements.
25: wĕkhol-ʾerets bāʾû bayaʿar wayhî ḏĕḇaš ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh — 'All the people came to the forest and there was honey on the ground.' The honey episode: yaʿar (H3293 — forest) with dĕḇaš (H1706 — honey, wild honey from forest bees). The honey flowing on the ground (ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh) suggests a large hive or possibly honeycomb that had fallen from trees. The Philistine territory in the Michmash highlands was known for forest coverage. The contrast with Saul's oath (v24: ʾārûr hāʾîš ʾăšer yōkhal leḥem ʿad-hāʿereḇ — 'cursed is the man who eats food until evening') was total: YHWH was providing abundant food to the soldiers precisely when Saul had prohibited eating — YHWH's provision contradicted Saul's oath.
26: vayyāḇōʾ hāʿām ʾel-hayyaʿar wehinnēh hēlēkh ḏāḇaš welōʾ-šāmah yādô ʾîš ʾel-pîw — 'When the people came to the forest, there was honey dripping, but no one put his hand to his mouth.' The visual poignancy: the honey was dripping (hēlēkh ḏāḇaš — flowing honey), visible and accessible, and yet the people's hands would not reach their mouths because of Saul's oath (kî-yārēʾ hāʿām ʾet-haššĕḇûʿāh — the people feared the oath). The word yārēʾ (H3372 — to fear, reverence) for the people's response to Saul's oath shows they took it seriously, even as the narrator implies the oath was foolish. The soldiers were exhausted from battle (v31: they were faint) yet denied the energy-giving food that YHWH had placed before them.
27: weʾet-ʾêyn-hayyaʿar weḏĕḇaš-ḥāyāh yônāṯān lōʾ šāmaʿ — 'Jonathan had not heard his father command the people with the oath.' The key narrative detail: Jonathan did not know about the oath (lōʾ šāmaʿ — he had not heard). He therefore reached out with the tip of his staff (wayyišlaḥ qētsēh hammattēh ʾăšer bĕyādô), dipped it in a honeycomb (wayyiṭbĕlēhû beyaʿar haddĕḇāš), ate (vayyāšeb yādô ʾel-pîw), and his eyes brightened (vattoʾōrnāh ʿênāyw). The brightening of eyes (ʾôr ʿênayyim — H215, H5869, to give light to the eyes) is a physiological effect of blood sugar restoration but also an idiom for gaining clarity and renewed energy. Jonathan's innocent transgression of an unknowingly unjust oath set the stage for the theological crisis that would follow.
28: vayyaʿan ʾîš mēhāʿām vayyōʾmer hiššābîaʿ hēšbîaʿ ʾāḇîkhā — 'One of the people answered: Your father strictly bound the people with an oath.' The doubled infinitive absolute (hiššābîaʿ hēšbîaʿ — strictly swore/bound) intensifies the obligation. The informant's tone is neutral — neither accusatory nor apologetic. Jonathan's response (v29-30) is the critical theological evaluation: ʿākhar ʾāḇî ʾet-hāʾārets (my father has troubled the land). The verb ʿāḵar (H5916 — to trouble, disturb, bring disaster upon — the same word for Achan who troubled Israel, Joshua 7:25; for Jephthah, Judges 11:35) is a severe judgment word. Jonathan attributed the battle's incomplete victory directly to Saul's oath: Israel was weakened when it should have been strengthened by the available food.
29: vayyōʾmer yônāṯān ʿākhar ʾāḇî ʾet-hāʾārets — 'Jonathan said: My father has brought trouble on the land.' The critique is remarkable — a son explicitly judging his father's military-theological decision as harmful. Jonathan's argument: rĕʾû nāʾ kî ʾōrû ʿênay kî ṭāʿamtî mĕʿaṭ ḏĕḇaš hazzeh (see how my eyes are brightened because I tasted a little of this honey). The empirical argument: the food improved his capacity and energy. The implication: an entire army fed would have been exponentially more effective in battle. Kî ʿal ʾap kî-lōʾ hārĕḇāh hayyôm hāʿām miśśelal ʾōyĕḇāyw (how much greater if the people had freely eaten from their enemies' spoil today!).
30: ʾap kî lūʾ ʾākhal ʾākhal hayyôm hāʿām miśśelal ʾōyĕḇāyw — 'How much better if the people had freely eaten from their enemies' spoil today.' The hypothetical (lûʾ — would that, if only — the particle of unfulfilled wish) expresses Jonathan's military assessment: a fed army would have killed more Philistines. Kî ʿattāh lōʾ-rāḇāh hammakkāh bapĕlishtîm (now the defeat of the Philistines has not been great). The victory was partial when it could have been total: the Philistines escaped rather than being destroyed. Saul's oath had limited what YHWH had begun through Jonathan — a military judgment delivered by the very man whose faith had initiated the battle.
31: vayyakkû bayyôm hahûʾ bapĕlishtîm mimmikĕmāš ʾayyālônāh vayyāʿap hāʿām meʾōd — 'They struck the Philistines that day from Michmash to Aijalon, and the people were very faint.' The battle line ran from Michmash (the starting point) to Aijalon (H357 — the valley where Joshua commanded the sun to stand still, Joshua 10:12) — approximately 30 km of pursuit. The exhaustion was real: vayyāʿap hāʿām meʾōd (the people were very faint — H5774, H3966; yāʿēp means to be weary, exhausted, to fly/rush in exhaustion). The faintness proved Jonathan's point: denied food during a day of intense combat over 30 km, the soldiers were physically depleted when they should have been energized by the abundant honey YHWH had provided.
32: vayyāʿaš hāʿām ʾel-hašālāl vayyiqqĕḥû tsōʾn ûḇāqār ûḇnê bāqār vayyišḥăṭû ʾartsāh — 'The people pounced on the spoil and took sheep, oxen, and calves and slaughtered them on the ground.' Vayyāʿaš (H5860 — to rush/fly at, pounce on — from the root of a bird swooping on prey) captures the urgency of the starving soldiers' response. The critical violation: vayyōkhal hāʿām ʿal-haddām (the people ate with the blood) — blood still in the meat — violating the covenant law (Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 12:16,23; Genesis 9:4). The soldiers' hunger, created by Saul's oath, led directly to their breaking the blood prohibition. One foolish oath cascaded into a second transgression. Saul's orders had made his people sin — the opposite of a king's purpose.
33: vayyaggidû lĕšāʾûl lēʾmōr hinnēh hāʿām ḥōṭĕʾîm laYHWH lĕʾkhōl ʿal-haddām — 'They told Saul: The people are sinning against YHWH by eating with the blood.' The report reached Saul: ḥōṭĕʾîm laYHWH (sinning against YHWH — H2398 from H2399 ḥēṭ, sin/missing the mark). Saul's response was a practical corrective: bigĕdû ûgĕlēlû ʾēlay ʿettāh ʾeḇen gĕḏôlāh (roll a large stone to me right away). By providing a raised surface for proper slaughtering (with blood drainage), Saul tried to stop the sin mid-act. He appointed overseers (šōṭĕrîm) to ensure the animals were slaughtered properly. But the cause of the violation — his own oath — went unaddressed. The king managed the symptom while ignoring the disease.
34: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl pōtsû ḇāʿām waʾămartem lāhem haggîšû ʾēlay ʾîš šôrô ûmān ʾîš śēh — 'Saul said: Disperse among the people and say to them: Bring me now every man his ox and his sheep.' The ad hoc slaughterhouse: a large stone (ʾeḇen gĕḏôlāh) as the slaughtering platform, with overseers to ensure proper blood drainage. Vayyiḇīʾû kol-hāʿām ʾîš šôrô bĕyādô hallaylāh (all the people brought, each man his ox, that night). The practical emergency sacrifice allowed the soldiers to eat lawfully. But the Chronicler's note — vayyišḥāṭ šāmmāh (they slaughtered there) — shows Saul building his first altar. The altar-building was improvised rather than ordained: Saul reacting to a crisis he created rather than acting from covenant initiative.
35: vayyiḇen šāʾûl mizbēaḥ laYHWH ʾōtô heḥēl liḇnôt mizbēaḥ laYHWH — 'Saul built an altar to YHWH; it was the first altar he had built to YHWH.' The note ʾōtô heḥēl (it was the beginning/this was the first) is both a chronological note and a theological assessment: Saul's first altar was built in response to a crisis he caused. Compare David's altar-building in 2 Sam 24 — also in response to a crisis, but motivated by intercession for the people. Saul's altar was motivated by damage control. The verb bānāh (H1129 — to build) for altar construction anticipates Solomon's temple-building — but Saul's altar was ad hoc, not divinely commissioned. The contrast with Samuel's carefully maintained altar at Ramah (1 Samuel 7:17) is implicit.
36: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl nērdāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm laylāh — 'Saul said: Let us go down after the Philistines by night.' Saul's plan: a night pursuit (laylāh) to continue the offensive before the Philistines could regroup. The people's readiness: kōl-hayyāšār beʿênêkhā ʾăśēh (do what is good in your eyes). The priest's intervention: hannigĕšāh hălōm ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm (let us approach God here first — H5066). This anonymous priest's caution — consult YHWH before a night attack — represented the proper protocol. Saul finally agreed to consult. But the oracle was silent: welōʾ ʿānāhû YHWH bayyôm hahûʾ (YHWH did not answer him that day). Divine silence was itself a message: someone in the camp had violated a covenant term.
37: wayyišʾal šāʾûl beʾĕlōhîm haʾēredāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm — 'Saul inquired of God: Shall I go down after the Philistines?' The formal inquiry followed binary oracle format: haʾēredāh ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm hatittĕnēm bĕyad yisrāʾēl (shall I go down after the Philistines? will you give them into Israel's hand?). The silence (welōʾ ʿānāhû bayyôm hahûʾ) was interpreted correctly: something was wrong. VayyōʾmerʾelʾYHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾēl hāḇāh tāmîm (Saul said: Come near, all you leaders of the people; know and see how this sin has occurred today). The investigation began with the assumption that the sin was a human breach — likely the oath violation Saul had created, now to be identified through lot-casting.
38: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl iggĕšû hălōm kōl pinnôt hāʿām — 'Saul said: Come here, all you leaders of the people.' Pinnôt hāʿām (H6438 — corners/heads of the people, i.e., the leaders) were assembled for the investigation. The command: wĕdiʿû ûrĕʾû bāmmāh hāyĕṯāh haḥaṭṭāʾt hazzōʾt hayyôm (know and see in what this sin has occurred today). The lot-casting format for identifying hidden sin (cf. Joshua 7:14-18 — the Achan procedure) required eliminating groups systematically. Saul's self-confident investigation was ironic: he was about to uncover the deed of the son whose heroism had won the day, not knowing that his own oath-creation was the root cause of the crisis.
39: kî-ḥay YHWH hamōšîaʿ ʾet-yisrāʾēl — 'As YHWH lives, who saves Israel.' The divine oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives — H2416, H3068) was the strongest possible assertion. Saul swore that even if the violator was his own son Jonathan (kî bĕyônāṯān bĕnî yihyeh), he would die (mōt yāmût). The word hamōšîaʿ (who saves — H3467, the participle from yāšaʿ) is the same root as yĕšûʿāh and Yēšûaʿ/Joshua/Jesus. Saul swore by 'YHWH the Savior' that he would execute the hero who had just saved Israel — the theological irony of using YHWH's saving character as warranty for a death sentence on YHWH's instrument of salvation.
40: vayyōʾmer ʾel-kol-yisrāʾēl ʾattem tihyû lĕʾāḥāḏ wĕʾănî wĕyônāṯān bĕnî nīhyeh lĕʾāḥāḏ — 'You be on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on the other side.' The lot-casting procedure divided the community: people versus royal family. Vayyōmĕrû hāʿām ʾel-šāʾûl hakkōḇ ʾet-ʾăšer-ṭôḇ bĕʿênêkhā (the people said to Saul: Do what seems good in your eyes). Their compliance was passive — neither endorsing nor opposing Saul's investigation. The lot fell on the royal family, then further to Jonathan. The lot-casting (gôrāl — H1486, the divination by sacred lots) was understood as YHWH's judgment — its results were authoritative and binding, not subject to appeal.
41: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾēl hāḇāh tāmîm — 'Saul said to YHWH God of Israel: Give Urim.' The Urim-Thummim oracle: tāmîm (H8550 — Thummim, completeness, perfection) paired with ûrîm (H224 — Urim, lights, illumination) was the priestly binary oracle system. The specific query structure: 'If the guilt is in me or in Jonathan my son, give Urim; if the guilt is in the people, give Thummim.' The lot fell on Jonathan (vayyillākēd yônāṯān wĕšāʾûl — both were singled out first, then Jonathan alone). The oracle's precision confirmed Jonathan's deed — not as moral guilt (he acted in ignorance) but as the technical trigger that required resolution under Saul's oath.
42: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl hapîlû bênî ûḇên yônāṯān bĕnî — 'Saul said: Cast the lot between me and Jonathan my son.' The final casting narrowed to the two — father and son. Vayyillākēd yônāṯān (Jonathan was taken/caught). The word lākaḏ (H3920 — to be caught, trapped, taken — used for animals caught in traps, Psalms 9:15; Jeremiah 50:24) applied to Jonathan creates bitter irony: the hero who had 'caught' the Philistines by surprise was now himself 'caught' by his father's oath. The oracle identified the transgressor without judgment of intent — Saul's theological framework required execution regardless of ignorance.
43: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl ʾel-yônāṯān haggidāh-lî māh-ʿāśîtā — 'Saul said to Jonathan: Tell me what you have done.' Jonathan's confession was direct and undefended: ṭāʿōm ṭāʿamtî bekṣēh hammaṭṭeh ʾăšer bĕyādî mĕʿaṭ dĕḇaš hinnennî ʾāmût (I tasted a little honey with the tip of the staff I had in my hand. I am ready to die). The doubled infinitive absolute ṭāʿōm ṭāʿamtî (I surely tasted) acknowledges the act without minimizing it. Hinnennî ʾāmût (here I am; I will die) is Jonathan's acceptance of the oath's consequences, even knowing his innocence. His acceptance was not resignation but royal dignity — he had acted rightly, the oracle had spoken, the oath was binding. His courage in battle and his courage in judgment were of the same character.
44: vayyōʾmer šāʾûl kōh yaʿăśeh ʾĕlōhîm wĕkhōh yōsîp kî-mōt tāmût yônāṯān — 'Saul said: May God do thus and more also; you shall surely die, Jonathan.' The execution formula kōh yaʿăśeh ʾĕlōhîm wĕkhōh yōsîp (God do so and more so — the standard Hebrew oath formula invoking divine punishment) was followed by kî-mōt tāmût (for you shall surely die — the doubled infinitive absolute signaling certainty). Saul's rigidity is the narrative's darkest moment: the king prepared to execute the hero whose faith had won the day, because of an oath the hero had not heard. The people's rescue of Jonathan (v45) would demonstrate that Israel's moral judgment was sounder than Saul's legal formalism.
46: vayyaʿal šāʾûl mē ʾaḥărê pĕlishtîm wayyaʿălû pĕlishtîm limĕqômām — 'Saul withdrew from pursuing the Philistines, and the Philistines went to their own place.' The battle's incomplete conclusion: because of the oath crisis and Jonathan's near-execution, the pursuit was abandoned. The Philistines escaped to their territory (limĕqômām). The verb ʿālāh (to go up — H5927) for the Philistines 'going up to their place' may indicate they retreated to the coastal plain, lower in elevation than the Michmash highlands. The battle that Jonathan had initiated with extraordinary faith ended inconclusively — Saul's oath had truncated what YHWH had begun. The victory was real but not decisive; the Philistine threat would persist.
47: wĕšāʾûl laḵaḏ hammĕlûkhāh ʿal-yisrāʾēl — 'Saul took the kingship over Israel.' The summary of Saul's wars functions as a regal report, the kind preserved in the royal annals. The enemies he fought: môʾāḇ ûḇnê ʿammôn wĕʾeḏôm ûmalkê tsôḇāh ûḇapĕlishtîm (Moab, Ammonites, Edom, the kings of Zobah, and the Philistines). Ûḇĕkhol ʾăšer yipneh yarîšaʿ (wherever he turned he was victorious — literally 'he acted wickedly/was successful'). The difficult phrase yarîšaʿ (H7561 — a Hiphil form meaning 'he caused to be condemned/attacked'?) is usually translated as 'was victorious.' The catalog of enemies shows Saul's military range — multiple fronts simultaneously — even as the theological assessment of his reign was already compromised.
48: vayyaʿas ḥayil vayyakh ʾet-ʿămālēq vayyatssēl ʾet-yisrāʾēl miyaḏ šōsēhû — 'He gathered an army and struck the Amalekites and delivered Israel from those who plundered them.' Vayyaʿas ḥayil (he did valiantly/gathered a force — H2428 from H2428 ḥāyil, strength, army, wealth) summarizes Saul's military achievement. The Amalekite victory (vayyakh ʾet-ʿămālēq) is mentioned here briefly; the full account in 1 Samuel 15 will show the obedience failure that cost Saul the kingdom. But here the summary is positive: Saul did deliver Israel (vayyatssēl ʾet-yisrāʾēl) from her plunderers. The tension in the Saul narrative is that he was a genuine military leader whose accomplishments were real, but whose spiritual failures were equally real and ultimately disqualifying.
49: wayhî bĕnê-šāʾûl yônāṯān weyišwî ûmalki-šûaʿ — 'The sons of Saul were Jonathan, Ishvi, and Malchishua.' The family record preserves three sons (yônāṯān weyišwî ûmalki-šûaʿ) and two daughters (mêraḇ haggĕḏôlāh ûmîkhal haqqĕṭannāh — Merab the elder and Michal the younger). Michal would become David's wife (1 Samuel 18:20-28); Merab was promised to David but given to Adriel (1 Samuel 18:17-19). Išwî (H3440) is sometimes identified with Abinadab who died with Jonathan at Gilboa (1 Samuel 31:2). The family genealogy establishes the full context of Saul's household — the connections through which David's path to the throne would run.
50: wĕšem ʾēšet šāʾûl ʾăḥînōʿam bat-ʾăḥîmāʿats — 'The name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam daughter of Ahimaaz.' The royal household women are identified by patronymic (bat-ʾăḥîmāʿats — daughter of Ahimaaz). The commander of Saul's army: wĕšem śar-ṣĕḇāʾô ʾăḇnēr ben-nēr dôḏ šāʾûl (Abner son of Ner, Saul's uncle, was commander of his army). Abner would become a significant figure in the David-Saul succession struggle (2 Sam 2-3). His relationship to Saul — dôḏ (uncle) — placed him in the innermost royal circle. The information about Ner (Abner's father) and Kish (Saul's father) being sons of Abiel establishes the wider Benjamite clan network within which Saul's house operated.
51: wĕqîš ʾăḇî-šāʾûl wĕnēr ʾăḇî-ʾăḇnēr ben-ʾăḇîʾēl — 'Kish was the father of Saul, and Ner the father of Abner, son of Abiel.' The genealogical note connects the two branches of Saul's family: Kish (Saul's father) and Ner (Abner's father) were both sons of Abiel (ben-ʾăḇîʾēl). This means Saul and Abner were first cousins (not uncle-nephew as v50 implies — the word dôḏ can mean both). The genealogy validates Abner's military appointment: he was royal family, not merely a talented outsider. His loyalty to the house of Saul (2 Samuel 2:8-9 — placing Ish-bosheth on the throne) was kinship loyalty as much as political calculation.
52: wattihî hammilḥāmāh ḥăzāqāh ʿal-pĕlishtîm kōl-yĕmê šāʾûl — 'There was fierce war against the Philistines all the days of Saul.' The summary verse frames Saul's entire reign: chronic war (milḥāmāh ḥăzāqāh — fierce/hard war) against the Philistines was the defining context. The military personnel policy: wĕkhol-ʾîš gibbôr ʾăšer rāʾāh šāʾûl (every capable warrior that Saul saw), vayyeʾespēhû ʾēlāyw (he gathered him to himself). Saul's standing army recruitment created the military context into which David would enter: 1 Samuel 16:21 — 'David came to Saul and entered his service.' The final verse of 1 Samuel 14 is the hinge between the Saul cycle and the David cycle: the chronic war that Saul could not win would be the context in which YHWH raised up his chosen king.
